Before we move on to the simulation, I want to wrap up the main points from this course and give you a real-life example for the skills we've discussed so far. I'm sure you've been in a situation where you were emailing someone that you're trying to do business with and you got no response. Then you try to turn on your charm and send the most courteous follow-up email that anyone has ever sent in the history of the world only to get ignored again. I mean no one likes rejection, but being ignored is disheartening. I've got good news. Next time this happens to you, use a no to provoke a response with this one-sentence email. Send this line. Have you given up on this project? Nothing more, nothing less. And then sit there and wait. I'm not kidding. Our average response time for this message is three minutes. This question works because it plays on your counterpart's natural human aversion to loss. The no answer that this email provokes offers your counterpart the feeling of safety and control while avoiding the prospect of loss. The natural tendency of the person you're emailing will be to immediately reply and rescue the situation. You might be afraid your counterpart will perceive this email as being rude, but they don't because they are replying from the safety of no. The good news is that they will immediately reply. The bad news is you've probably been phishing or pushing too much for yes, and that's why they stopped communicating. You have to be prepared to follow up with a series of labels that will draw them out or what question, such as, what is standing in our way? I want you to try this next time you find yourself in a situation where you're being ignored. You might feel that this tool and the others we discussed in this course will be difficult to apply to your life. I encourage you to test drive them to find out for yourself. Break the habit of pushing for yes from people. They will get defensive and disengage from what you have to say. The word no is not failure. It is not the opposite of yes. Train yourself to hear it in one of its many other forms. They usually say the word no as a reflex and really want to be able to process all of the details so they can make the most informed decision possible. No is often where the negotiation starts. So don't aim for yes from the start. It's almost always counterfeit. People say it is a way to escape the conversation. Saying the word no on the other hand makes the speaker feel safe, secure, and in control. So trigger it as soon as possible. By expressing what they don't want, your counterpart is allowed to define their space and gain confidence in listening to you. You can also intentionally mislabel their emotion to get a no. Successful persuasion is not about how smooth or forceful you are. These will create a backlash and will waste your time. The key is to help the other person discover the solution you want as their idea. Don't beat them with logic or brute force. Instead ask them no oriented questions and use labels to open the paths to your goals. Remember it's not about you. Leave your ego at the door.
